title: Hurricane Shutter & Impact Window Installation
verdict: Acquisition Strong Conviction. Regulated, recurring demand driven by building codes and insurance requirements. Florida market alone is massive with expanding code requirements. 35-45% gross margins on installation with insurance-driven demand that does not depend on discretionary spending.
verdict_detail: Hurricane protection installation serves coastal markets where building codes and insurance mandates create non-discretionary demand. Florida, the Gulf Coast, and coastal Carolinas require hurricane-rated protection on new construction and renovations. Impact windows ($5,000-25,000 per home) and hurricane shutters ($2,000-8,000) are mandatory upgrades that insurance companies incentivize with premium discounts of 10-40%. The acquisition thesis targets established installers with contractor relationships, building code expertise, and insurance company partnerships. This is a regulated recurring business — every new building permit and insurance renewal creates demand.
action: Identify hurricane shutter and impact window installers in Florida and Gulf Coast markets with 3+ year track records and aging owners. Focus on companies with strong insurance company referral relationships.
why_works: Building codes and insurance requirements create non-discretionary demand. Florida market alone generates billions in hurricane protection spending annually. 35-45% gross margins on installation. Insurance premium discounts (10-40%) pay for the installation over time, making the sale easier. Every new construction and renovation requires compliance, creating recurring pipeline. Climate change is expanding the market northward.
why_fails: Seasonal demand peaks before hurricane season (May-July) with a long tail. General contractors and window companies can offer hurricane products as add-ons. Insurance company requirements change and can reduce mandated upgrades. Material costs (impact glass, aluminum) fluctuate with commodity prices. Post-hurricane demand spikes followed by long quiet periods create revenue volatility.
validate_first: Call 5 insurance agents in target market. Ask: what hurricane protection do they require for new policies? What discounts do they offer? Which installation companies do they recommend? The answers map the demand pipeline.
category_def: Hurricane shutter and impact window installation provides code-compliant storm protection for residential and commercial properties in coastal hurricane zones. Products include accordion shutters, roll-down shutters, impact-resistant windows, storm panels, and hurricane fabric. Revenue comes from new installation ($2,000-25,000 per project), replacement of aging systems, and repair after storm damage. The business requires contractor licensing, building code knowledge, and relationships with insurance companies and building departments.
demand_drivers: Florida building code (the strictest in the nation) requires hurricane-rated protection on all new construction and major renovations in wind-borne debris regions. Insurance companies mandate or strongly incentivize hurricane protection with premium discounts. Post-hurricane repair and replacement creates demand spikes. Aging housing stock requires system replacement every 15-20 years. Climate change is expanding hurricane zones northward, creating new markets in the Carolinas and mid-Atlantic.
geo_fit: Florida is the primary market (by far), followed by Gulf Coast (Texas, Louisiana, Mississippi, Alabama), coastal Carolinas, and to a lesser extent the mid-Atlantic coast. Miami-Dade and Broward County have the strictest codes and highest demand. Secondary Florida markets (Tampa, Jacksonville, Panhandle) are growing rapidly.
core_buyer: Homeowners in coastal hurricane zones, typically triggered by insurance requirement, new home purchase, or post-storm repair. Secondary: commercial property owners and HOAs managing multi-unit buildings. Real estate agents refer buyers who need compliance for closing.
revenue_engine: New construction compliance (builders must install hurricane protection). Insurance-mandated upgrades (homeowners installing to get coverage or discounts). Post-storm repair and replacement (insurance claim-driven). Aging system replacement (15-20 year product lifecycle). Renovation and addition triggers (building permit requires compliance on the entire structure).
hard_part: Navigating the building code and permit process is the hardest operational challenge. Each jurisdiction has different requirements, and the Miami-Dade County product approval process (NOA - Notice of Acceptance) is the gold standard that most other jurisdictions reference. Installers who understand the code and can handle permitting efficiently have a genuine competitive advantage.
why_path: Acquisition is preferred because the contractor licensing, building code expertise, insurance company relationships, and permit navigation capabilities take years to develop. An established installer with 5+ years of history and strong local relationships is worth the acquisition premium. Starting from scratch requires 1-2 years of licensing, relationship building, and code education before meaningful revenue.
economics_intro: Hurricane protection installation generates 35-45% gross margins. A typical residential impact window installation of $15,000 has $6,000-8,000 in materials (impact windows, hardware), $2,000-3,000 in labor (1-2 days for a 2-person crew), leaving $4,000-7,000 in gross profit. Hurricane shutters have slightly lower average project values ($4,000-8,000) but similar margin profiles.
econ_insight: The key metric is revenue per installation crew per month. A two-person crew completing 8-12 installations per month at $10,000 average project value generates $80-120K in monthly revenue. During peak season (May-July), crews can run at 120% capacity. Off-season (Nov-Feb) drops to 50-60%.
econ_rows: Completed projects x average project value
Windows vs shutters vs panels margin comparison
Below 30% blended = pricing or material cost problem
Percentage of leads from insurance channels
Days from estimate to permit approval to install
unit_econ_intro: Three-case model for acquiring a hurricane protection installer. Downside is post-acquisition crew attrition. Base case reflects stable Florida operations. Upside assumes insurance referral expansion and geographic growth.
buybox: 3+ years in business with active contractor license
Building code expertise and efficient permit process
2+ installation crews with experienced crew leaders
Warehouse for material storage and staging
disqualifiers: No contractor license. Revenue below $500K. Single crew dependency. No insurance company relationships. Located in non-hurricane zone. Pending code violations or legal issues.
competitive_intro: Competition comes from general contractors offering hurricane protection as add-on, dedicated hurricane shutter and window companies, window replacement companies (Window World, Renewal by Andersen) adding impact products, and DIY hurricane panels from big-box stores. The advantage of a dedicated specialist is code expertise, efficient permitting, insurance company trust, and product range.
comp_whitespace_summary: Whitespace in insurance company direct partnerships (preferred installer programs), smart/automated hurricane protection (motorized roll-downs with app control), and expanding markets (Carolinas, mid-Atlantic) where hurricane codes are being adopted.
comp_rows: Hurricane protection is not their specialty, slower permitting
Expensive, not hurricane-code focused, long lead times
Code expertise, efficient permitting, insurance relationships
Premium pricing, limited availability during peak season
Not code-compliant in many jurisdictions, no warranty, insurance may not accept
valuation: 3-4x SDE for established installers. A company at $1.2M revenue with $178K SDE acquires for $534-712K. Working capital (inventory) adds $50-100K. Strong insurance referral relationships can justify premium multiples.
financing: SBA 7(a) for acquisition. Material inventory financing or supplier terms. SBA 504 for warehouse/office real estate. Seller note for 15-25% alignment.
capital_rules: Never pay more than 4x SDE. Verify insurance referral relationships transfer post-sale. Budget for material inventory on top of purchase price. Require 6-month transition with owner maintaining insurance and building department relationships. Model at 85% of trailing revenue for post-acquisition adjustment.
cust_diligence: Call 5 insurance agents: which installers do they recommend and why?
Survey recent customers: satisfaction, referral likelihood, insurance discount received
Check building department relationships: how fast are permits approved?
Review project pipeline: what is the backlog of signed contracts?
Assess referral sources: what percentage comes from insurance vs builders vs direct?
oper_diligence: Verify contractor license status and any violations
Audit installation quality: visit 5 recent installations
Assess crew quality: experience, turnover, safety record
Review warehouse and material inventory: condition, organization, dead stock
Check supplier relationships: pricing, payment terms, exclusivity
fin_diligence: 3 years tax returns and monthly P&L
Revenue by product line (windows, shutters, panels, repairs)
Accounts receivable: insurance claim payment cycles
Material cost trends and supplier pricing stability
Worker classification and insurance coverage adequacy
growth_diligence: Geographic expansion: adjacent Florida markets or new states adopting hurricane codes
Smart hurricane protection (motorized, app-controlled) as premium offering
Commercial properties: hotels, condos, office buildings
ai_intro: AI helps with project estimation (automated quoting from property measurements and satellite imagery), permit processing (auto-filling jurisdiction-specific permit applications), and insurance claim management (documenting damage and expediting claims).
ai_advantage: Project estimation is the biggest AI opportunity. An AI tool that takes property measurements and automatically generates a code-compliant product specification and quote could reduce estimating time from 2 hours to 15 minutes per project, allowing more estimates and higher close rates.
ai_rows: AI quoting from property data and measurements
Estimating time from 2 hours to 15 minutes
AI damage documentation and claim processing
Demand prediction for materials before hurricane season
Targeting homeowners with expiring systems or new insurance requirements
risks: Seasonal demand concentration: 60%+ of revenue in May-September
Post-hurricane demand spikes followed by multi-year quiet periods
Building code changes could reduce or increase requirements unpredictably
Material cost volatility: aluminum and glass prices affect margins
Insurance company policy changes could reduce mandated upgrades
Crew safety: installation at height and in coastal conditions
Competition from national window companies entering the impact market
kill_criteria: No contractor license obtainable. Target market not in hurricane zone. Insurance companies not requiring or incentivizing protection. Revenue below $500K. Single crew with no scalability path.
market_stats: Market expanding to Carolinas and mid-Atlantic
market_text: The Florida hurricane protection market exceeds $1B annually, driven by the nation's strictest building codes, insurance mandates, and the nation's highest hurricane exposure. Demand is non-discretionary for new construction and major renovations in wind-borne debris regions. The market is expanding as coastal Carolina and mid-Atlantic states adopt similar codes. Impact windows represent the premium product category with higher average project values and growing consumer preference for permanent protection over deployable shutters.
market_trend: Strong positive. Florida building code continues to tighten. Insurance companies are increasingly requiring hurricane protection rather than merely incentivizing it. Climate change is expanding the geographic market. Impact windows are gaining share over shutters as consumers prefer permanent solutions. Post-2020 building boom created a large installed base that will need replacement in 15-20 years.
op_wins: Non-discretionary demand driven by code and insurance
Insurance premium discounts pay for installations over time, making the sale easier
Building code expertise is a genuine competitive moat
Growing market as codes expand geographically
Product replacement cycle creates recurring demand
Post-hurricane repair provides demand spikes with insurance funding
op_losses: Over-relying on seasonal peak revenue without planning for off-season
Underpricing to win volume when margins are the key to sustainability
Not building insurance company referral partnerships
Ignoring permit process efficiency (slow permitting kills conversion)
Failing to maintain crew quality and safety standards
Not diversifying beyond shutters into the higher-value impact window segment
op_quote: The installers who win are the ones who make the insurance and permit process invisible for the homeowner. If the customer has to navigate code compliance themselves, you have failed at your core value proposition.
comp_whitespace: Insurance company preferred installer programs: formal partnerships for direct referral
Smart/automated hurricane protection: motorized roll-downs with app control at premium pricing
Expanding geographic markets: Carolinas and mid-Atlantic adopting hurricane codes
Commercial properties: hotels, condos, offices needing bulk installation
New construction partnerships: homebuilders wanting hurricane compliance as standard
Emergency response capability: post-storm rapid repair and replacement service
score_yes: Non-discretionary demand from codes and insurance
Florida market is massive and growing
Product replacement cycle creates recurring demand
score_caveats: Seasonal demand concentration creates cash flow challenges
Crew safety and labor intensity are real operational challenges
Score may underweight the geographic concentration risk
experts: Revenue, margins, seasonal patterns, insurance referral dynamics
What they require, what they recommend, discount structures
Code enforcement, common violations, what makes a good installer
expert_calls: Revenue? Margins? Peak season? Insurance referral share? Biggest challenge?
What hurricane protection do you require? Discount structure? Preferred installers?
Miami-Dade, Broward, or Hillsborough building dept
Common code violations? What makes a good installer? Permit process efficiency?
Decision factors? Price paid? Insurance discount received? Satisfaction?
roadmap30: Map all hurricane protection installers in target Florida markets
Identify established companies (5+ years) with aging owners
Research contractor licensing requirements for target jurisdiction
Build relationships with 5+ insurance agents for referral pipeline intel
Visit 3 installers as a prospective customer to assess quality and process
Research SBA lending for construction/home service acquisitions
kill30: No viable acquisition targets. Market dominated by large players. Insurance requirements weakening in target area.
roadmap60: Request trailing 12-month financials and project pipeline
Verify contractor license, insurance referral relationships, and building department standing
Assess crew quality: tenure, safety record, productivity
Build three-case model with seasonal revenue adjustment
kill60: Targets have unrealistic pricing. License issues or pending violations. Crew quality concerns. Insurance referral relationships non-transferable.
roadmap90: Implement project estimation technology for faster quoting
Expand insurance company preferred installer partnerships
Add impact window product line if shutter-only
Target 20% revenue growth within 12 months through expanded referral network
kill90: Diligence reveals code violations, license issues, or untransferable insurance relationships. Walk away.
final_rec: Hurricane shutter and impact window installation is a high-conviction acquisition play for Florida-based operators. Non-discretionary demand from building codes and insurance mandates creates recession-resistant revenue. The code expertise and permit navigation capability are genuine moats. Best targets are established installers in high-growth Florida markets with strong insurance referral pipelines. Impact windows represent the premium growth segment within this category.
next_actions: Map installers in target Florida markets
Talk to 5 insurance agents about referral dynamics
Build financial model with seasonal adjustment
Identify acquisition targets with aging owners
dan_decision: Is Dan in or willing to relocate to a Florida coastal market? This play is geographically locked to hurricane zones — it does not work in Boston or Denver.
